coredata.dc:title: Estimation of SoH and internal resistances of Lithium ion battery based on LSTM network
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.dc:creator: Nguyen Van, Chi
Quang, Duy Ta
coredata.dc:description: State of Health (SoH) and internal resistances, including the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) resistance and charge transfer resistance, are important parameters that change in the long-term representation of the aging state of Lithium-ion batteries. Using long short-term memory (LSTM) network, a neural network with the ability to remember long-term data features, this paper presents a method for estimating SoH and internal resistances of Lithium-ion batteries using LSTM network with deep learning mechanism. Based on experimental data including voltage, current, temperature with 03 charge/discharge scenarios and measuring impedance, input/output data structure is set up to reflect aging features used for estimating SoH and internal resistances by LSTM. The first LSTM network is designed to estimate SoH, then the data including current, voltage, temperature and estimated SoH will be used to estimate the SEI resistance and charge transfer resistance by the second LSTM network. With this structure, the estimation of internal resistances in practice will become simpler as it does not require measuring capacity and impedance spectroscopy. Comparing the estimation errors using LSTM and FNN with 03 performance metrics including mean absolute percentage error (MAPE), mean percentage error (MPE) and root mean square error (RMSE) shows that the estimation results of SoH and internal resistances of the cell by LSTM have higher accuracy than the estimation by Feedforward Neural Network (FNN).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery
State of health
LSTM
Internal resistances
Deep learning

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the changes corresponding to the capacity degradation during charge/discharge cycles of cell: a) charge voltage; b)charge current; c) temperature of the cell; d) discharge voltage; e) discharge current; f) capacity (plotted by using the battery data of NASA, https://data.nasa.gov/dataset/Li-ion-Battery-Aging-Datasets/uj5r-zjdb).
Representation of a typical LiB cell EIS measurement presented in a complex impedance plot [32].

The variation of the resistances
R
e
and
R
ct
as a function of the cell capacity of 165 discharge test cycles (plotted by using the battery data of NASA, https://data.nasa.gov/dataset/Li-ion-Battery-Aging-Datasets/uj5r-zjdb).
The structure for estimating SoH and internal resistances using LSTM network.
The structure of the network node n in the LSTM network.
Illustration of organizing and normalizing experimental data to create input/output data for training LSTM networks for estimating SoH.
Contour of the normalized input data used to estimate SoH with M = 35, corresponding to the output data at the charging cycles.
Illustration of organizing and normalizing experimental data to create input/output data for training LSTM networks for estimating internal resistences.

Contour of the normalized input data used to estimate
R
e
and
R
ct
with
M = 35
, corresponding to the output data.
Setup of LSTM network by Matlab to estimate SoH and internal resistances.
The graphical structure of the LSTM network used for SoH estimation.
The graphical structure of the LSTM network used for internal resistances estimation.
The estimated SoH by using proposed LSTM model of the cell 1 compared with the actual SoH (measured by charging cycles experiment) and 95 % confidence interval.
Error between the estimated SoH and the real SoH (measured by charging cycles experiment) of cell 1.
Q-Q plot of SoH estimation error for cell 1, two datasets have similar distributions, the points on the plot follow a straight line.
The estimated SoH by using proposed LSTM model of the cell 2 compared with the actual SoH, SoH estimated by FNN and 95 % confidence interval.
The estimated SEI resistance by using proposed LSTM model of the cell 2 compared with the real SEI resistance (measured by charging cycles experiment) and 95 % confidence interval.

The estimated charge transfer resistance by using proposed LSTM model of the cell 2 compared with the real charge transfer resistance (measured by charging cycles experiment) and 95 % confidence interval.
Comparison of 3 error performance metrics of estimated SoH for cell 2 between LSTM and FNN.
Three error performance metrics of SEI resitance estimation.
Three error performance metrics of charge transfer resitance estimation.

head.ce:title: Estimation of SoH and internal resistances of Lithium ion battery based on LSTM network

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Thai Nguyen University of Technology, Viet Nam

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Vinh Phuc College of Economics and Technology, Viet Nam

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Vinh Phuc College of Economics and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Vinh Phuc College of Economics and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: State of Health (SoH) and internal resistances, including the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) resistance and charge transfer resistance, are important parameters that change in the long-term representation of the aging state of Lithium-ion batteries. Using long short-term memory (LSTM) network, a neural network with the ability to remember long-term data features, this paper presents a method for estimating SoH and internal resistances of Lithium-ion batteries using LSTM network with deep learning mechanism. Based on experimental data including voltage, current, temperature with 03 charge/discharge scenarios and measuring impedance, input/output data structure is set up to reflect aging features used for estimating SoH and internal resistances by LSTM. The first LSTM network is designed to estimate SoH, then the data including current, voltage, temperature and estimated SoH will be used to estimate the SEI resistance and charge transfer resistance by the second LSTM network. With this structure, the estimation of internal resistances in practice will become simpler as it does not require measuring capacity and impedance spectroscopy. Comparing the estimation errors using LSTM and FNN with 03 performance metrics including mean absolute percentage error (MAPE), mean percentage error (MPE) and root mean square error (RMSE) shows that the estimation results of SoH and internal resistances of the cell by LSTM have higher accuracy than the estimation by Feedforward Neural Network (FNN).

ce:section.ce:para:
Lithium batteries are now known as an essential component in mobile electronic devices, energy storage systems for electric vehicles, and energy distribution systems. They play an increasingly important role in the field of energy storage because of their advantages over other types of batteries, such as high energy density, long service life, no memory effect, and so on. With the global carbon reduction strategy, electric and hybrid vehicles will boom in this decade, so the demand for Lithium batteries for electric vehicles will be even greater. The most important thing for customers when using electric vehicles is to know how much energy is left in the battery or how far they can travel reliably and accurately at the current time. In other words, the battery management system (BMS) needs to measure and calculate the accurate value of the state of charge (SoC) of the battery pack and provide it to the user. SoC is a parameter representing the amount of remaining electrical charge in the battery cell, which depends on many factors such as current, voltage, temperature, and especially the degree of aging of the battery cell. With the same SoC level, a new battery cell will allow the vehicle to travel a longer distance than an old battery cell. The charging time to reach 100 % SoC for an old cell will also be shorter than the charging time for a new cell. All of the above characteristics come from the aging of the battery over time, when the usage time of the cell increases, the actual capacity of the cell will decrease compared to the new cell capacity. In reality, in terms of electrochemical, this aging phenomenon can be caused by the thickening of the SEI layer, lithium corrosion, and loss of interaction during use. SEI allows the transportation of Li+ and blocks electrons to prevent further electrolytic decomposition and ensure the continuation of electrochemical reactions. Studies have also shown that the loss of free ions increases the internal resistance and raises the temperature of the battery during operation [1–3]. Due to the poor conductivity of the deposition layer, the excessive growth of SEI film increases the internal resistance. Generally, resistance growth and capacity decay are two direct consequences of the aging process, to represent the degree of aging or the degree of use of the battery cell, the concept of SoH is used [4–6], which is defined by formula (1):
(1)
SoH
k
=
Q
max
t
Q
rate
100 %
where
Q
max
t
is the maximum capacity that can be used at time k ,
Q
rate
is the capacity of a new cell when it leaves the factory. As the cell is used for a longer time
Q
max
t
decreases compared to
Q
rate
, therefore SoH decreases relative to 100 %.

SoH is a long-term variable parameter, and the degradation of SoH is closely related to the changes of internal resistances within the battery during usage. Estimating SoH and the internal resistances is crucial in BMS applications, as it helps to improve the accuracy of estimating SoC (a short-term variable parameter) based on an equivalent circuit model, to control the charging/discharging process and thermal generation to minimize capacity degradation, ensure safety, and prolong the lifespan of the battery cell. Additionally, estimating SoH provides information on the aging process for predicting the remaining lifespan of the cell and the degradation of materials used in battery manufacturing, among others [7,8].

There are currently several methods for estimating SoH, among which the commonly used ones are the Kalman filter (KF), Extended Kalman filter (EKF), and Sigma Point Kalman filter (SPKF) based on either an equivalent circuit model or an electrochemical model. Using an equivalent circuit model is difficult to ensure accuracy because the parameters of the equivalent circuit model need to be updated over time [9–14]. Estimating SoH based on an electrochemical model, represented by a set of differential equations describing the changes in electrochemical factors of the cell, yields more accurate results, however it is difficult to implement and increases the computational burden when deployed on hardware [15,16]. In addition, there are also methods for estimating SoH based on variational modal decomposition (VMD), particle filter (PF), integrated Gaussian process regression (GPR), particle swarm optimization (PSO), and broad learning system (BLS) [17–19]. These methods apply various computation algorithms to estimate SoH with high accuracy, but the computational load remains one of the main challenges.

Currently, in many studies, the estimation of SoH is performed based on an experimental data approach using neural networks. The advantage of the data approach is that it uses operational data to estimate SoH based on a prior learning process. This approach does not require much mathematical modeling of the capacity degradation process and the aging mechanisms that occur inside the battery. The accuracy of the SoH estimation depends on the structure of the neural network and the training algorithm. In documents [19-21], the authors use support vector machine (SVM) to estimate SoH. However, these algorithms require a large amount of historical data to be able to describe various forms of battery capacity degradation. In cases where the data of battery degradation has a high nonlinear characteristic, the accuracy of the SoH estimation may become less precise. In document [22], Liu and colleagues use an recurrent neural network (RNN) with additional capabilities of adaptive repetition to estimate SoH based on the dynamic state of the battery. Based on experimental battery data, the authors showed that the SoH estimation becomes less accurate as the number of charge/discharge cycles increases and the phenomenon of capacity self-recovery occurs at some points in the battery's life [23–27].

Recently, studies in [28–31] have proposed using LSTM neural networks and combining LSTM with other learning models such as gated recurrent unit (GRU) to improve long-term SoH estimation accuracy with less training data, achieving promising results.

Following the trend of using LSTM for estimating SoH, this paper proposes a structure using LSTM network to estimate SoH and internal resistances of a cell based on data. The data, including voltage, current, and temperature, are used to estimate SoH with the first LSTM network, then the data of current, voltage, temperature, and SoH estimated by the first LSTM network are used to estimate SEI resistance and charge transfer resistance with the second LSTM network. With this structure, by using estimated SoH, estimating internal resistances becomes simpler as it does not require capacitance and impedance spectroscopy measurements in practice, which can only be performed in a laboratory with specialized equipment, and are not suitable for commercial products. Comparing the estimation errors using LSTM and FNN with 3 performance metrics: MAPE, MPE, and RMSE, shows that the accuracy of SoH and internal resistance estimation with LSTM is higher than with FNN.

The rest of this paper is organized as follows. Section 2 describes estimating SoH and internal resistances using LSTM network. We present the results of SoH and internal resistances estimation in Section 3, followed by the conclusion in Section 4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Estimating soh and internal resistances using LSTM network
Correlation change between capacity and internal resistances of cell by charge/dischage cycles
ce:section.ce:para: Natural aging phenomenon occurs due to electrochemical reactions inside the Lithium-Ion battery. Fig. 1 below illustrates the change in voltage, current, and temperature of the cell corresponding to the capacity degradation after 600 charge/discharge cycles.

Fig. 2 illustrates the interplay between the electrochemical impedance and the degree of aging of the cell, where L represents the impedance at high frequencies, W is the characteristic element of the diffusion process,
R
e
//
C
p
e
is the impedance at medium frequencies, and
R
ct
//
C
p
ct
is the impedance at low frequencies. Charge transfer resistance
R
ct
is a measure of the difficulty encountered when an electron is shifted from one atom or compound to another atom or compound,
R
e
is resitance of the SEI film. According to recent studies, the two resistances
R
e
and
R
ct
are the parameters most affected by the aging process [19].

Fig. 3 illustrates the variation of the resistances
R
e
and
R
ct
with the cell capacity over 165 discharge test cycles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The structure proposed for estimating SoH and the internal resistances
ce:section.ce:para:
The structure for estimating SoH and the internal resistances of the cell is described in Fig. 4, where the input data includes current, voltage, and temperature; the first LSTM network is used to estimate SoH, the second LSTM network uses the input data like the first LSTM network and the estimated SoH to estimate the SEI layer resistance
R
e
and the charge transfer resistance
R
ct
.

The nodes
n , n = 1,2 , … ,
N
h
,
in the LSTM neural network have a structure as shown in Fig. 5, where
x
n
is the normalized input including voltage, current, and temperature of the cell,
c
n
and
h
n
are the state output and hidden output of the network node n , respectively. These outputs are recursively used to estimate SoH and the internal resistances of the battery cell.
c
n − 1
and
h
n − 1
are the two outputs of the previous network node
n − 1
. The σ symbol represents the sigmoid activation function with a matrix output, and tanh is the hyperbolic tangent function with a vector output. Each LSTM network node has three gates: the forget gate
f
n
, the input gate
i
n
and the output gate
o
n
. These gates are implemented by a regular neural network and act as filters of information, functions, and update formulas for the gates are as follows:
•
Input gate: Control the update rate of new state according to formula (2):
(2)
i
n
= σ (
W
i
[
h
n − 1
,
x
n
] +
b
i
)
•
Forget gate: Control the forgetting rate of old state according to formula (3):
(3)
f
n
= σ (
W
f
[
h
n − 1 ,
x
n
] +
b
f
)
•
Output gate: Control the update rate of state according to formula (4):
(4)
o
n
= σ (
W
o
[
h
n − 1
,
x
n
] +
b
o
)
where
W
i
,
b
i
,
W
f
,
b
f
,
W
o
,
b
o
are the weight and bias matries.

Each network node n has a vector
c
̃
n
determined through a hyperbolic tangent function centered at 0, with a gradient distribution to prevent vanishing phenomena.
c
̃
n
is a value used to adjust the update rate of the new state of the state cell, which is calculated using the following formula (5), where
W
c
and
b
c
are weight matrix and bias vector, respectively.
(5)
c
̃
n
= tanh (
W
c
[
h
n − 1
,
x
n
] +
b
c
)

The output of the network node's state
c
n
is calculated by formula (6), consisting of two components: the previous state component of the cell to be forgotten
f
n
*
c
n − 1
, and the new state component to be updated
i
n
*
c
̃
n
:
(6)
c
n
=
f
n
*
c
n − 1
+
i
n
*
c
̃
n

The hidden state output of network node is calculated by formula (7):
(7)
h
n
=
o
n
* tanh (
c
n
)

The parameters of LSTM network node are bias vectors
b
i
,
b
f
,
b
c
,
b
0
and weight matrices
W
i
,
W
f
,
W
c
,
W
0
of 04 layers.

From the above formulas, we see that
f
n
< 0
,
i
n
< 1 ,
o
n
< 1 ,
therefore we can see that
h
n
,
c
̃
n
are quite similar to an RNN network, so they are capable of remembering short-term information, while
c
n
is like a transmission belt on the RNN network model to select which important remote information will be sent in and used later as needed. Therefore, the LSTM network is capable of remembering long-term SoH and resistance information. We use the battery datasets of NASA Prognostics Center of Excellence Data Repository [33]. Data preparation for estimating SoH and resistance was conducted for 02 Samsung 22P 18650 battery cells with the negative electrode material with the following nominal parameters: operating voltage of 3.6–3.7 V; nominal capacity of 2200 mAh; charging cut-off voltage of 4.20 V ± 0.05 V; maximum discharge current of 10 A; not to be discharged below 2.5 V; diameter: 18.3 mm; height: 64.8 mm; weight: 43.3 g.

The battery cells were tested in 3 scenarios of charging, discharging, and measuring resistance using the ICA method. The charging scenario was carried out with a constant current charging mode of 1.5 A until the cell voltage reached 4.2 V, followed by a constant voltage charging mode until the current dropped to 20 mA. The discharging scenario was conducted with a discharge current of 2 A until the cell voltages reached 2.7 V for cell 1, 2.5 V for cell 2. The cells were operated at an ambient temperature of 24 °C. The complex impedance measurement scenario was obtained after performing 270 experimental resistance measurements in the frequency range from 1 Hz to 1 kHz.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The data normalization for estimating SoH
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of data for estimating SoH is a problem that needs to be specifically considered, so that the data can accurately reflect the characteristics of capacity degradation. Therefore, from the data obtained in the discharge scenario, we arrange the data for the cell as follows.

The voltage, current, and temperature data obtained from the discharge scenarios are divided into M reasonable ranges. The data in these ranges are then averaged and standardized within the range of 0–1. The general normalization formula for the data series X ( X can be current, voltage, temperature, or resistance,
X = { x
1
, x
2
, … x
N
}
, N is the number of sampled data points) is:
(8)
x
norm
k
=
x
k
−
x
min
x
max
−
x
min
, k = 1 , 2 , … , N
where
x
min
and
x
max
are respectively the minimum and maximum values of the X series . .

Illustration of the method of sorting and normalizing experimental voltage data to create data for training SoH estimation networks is shown in Fig. 6. The voltage data used for estimating the final SoH is a table with the number of rows equal to the number of test cycles, the number of columns is the number of divided ranges M , corresponding to the average voltage value of that range. Similarly for current and temperature data, finally, combining the 3 tables of voltage, current, and temperature data, we obtain the input data matrix, and the measured capacity values at the cycles are the output data. The illustration of arranging the input/output data for training the LSTM network to estimate SoH is described in Fig. 6. Fig. 7 shows the contour of the normalized input data used to estimate SoH with
M = 35
corresponding to the output data.

Therefore, the data used to train the LSTM network for SoH estimation includes an input matrix of size
N
c
× 3 M
, and an output vector of capacity data in the charging scenario, with a size of
N
c
× 1
. SoH is determined from the measured capacity using formula (1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The data normalization for estimating internal resistences
ce:section.ce:para:
After obtaining the resistance data,
R
e
and
R
ct
are determined as the polarization resistance and the Warburg impedance, respectively, which are scalar values representing the low-frequency region. These are the two main factors affecting long-term SoC [34]. The assumption between two measurement intervals to collect data is that the cell resistance remains constant. Data, including current, voltage, temperature, and cell capacity, are collected form three different operating profiles of charging, discharging and impedance scenarios at room temperature. The input data includes current, voltage, temperature, and capacity, while the output data includes
R
e
and
R
ct
. Fig. 8 and Fig. 9 show illustration of organizing and normalizing experimental data to create input/output data for training LSTM networks for estimating internal resistences and contour of the normalized input data used to estimate
R
e
and
R
ct
with M= 35, corresponding to the output data, respectively.

The total number of data acquisition cycles is
N
c
+
N
d
, and the input data matrix has a size of
( N
c
+
N
d
) × 4 M
. Assuming the impedance measurement points are k , the output data includes
R
e
and
R
ct
at the measurement points of
k − 1
and k , which are obtained from the data at point k . Therefore, the output data is a matrix with a size of
( N
c
+
N
d
) × 2
.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Setup the LSTM for estimation SoH and internal resistances
ce:section.ce:para: Using Matlab R2020 software, the Deep Network Designer tool is used to design an LSTM network for SoH estimation using Deep Learning, consisting of 4 layers as described in Fig. 10.

Fig. 11 illustrates the structure of the LSTM network used for SoH estimation, and the detailed arrangement of input and output data is also shown in this figure. Due to limited space in the drawing, the bias matrices for the input of the LSTM network and the regression matrix from the output back to the input of the next LSTM network are not depicted in detail, but represented by arrows.

The sizes of parameter matrices related to the partition interval M , and the number of hidden nodes
N
h
of the LSTM layer are as follows: InputSize=
3 M
, inputweight=
4
N
h
× 3 M
, Recurrentweight =
4 M ×
N
h
, bias=
4
N
h
× 1
.

Similarly to setting up an LSTM network for estimating SoH, the input size of the network is
4 M
for estimating
R
e
and
R
ct
, where the input data includes current, voltage, temperature, and capacity. The output data includes the equivalent series resistance
R
e
and the charge transfer resistance
R
ct
. The sizes of the parameter matrices with the number of hidden nodes
N
h
of the LSTM network are as follows: InputSize=
4 M
, inputweight=
4
N
h
× 4 M
, Recurrentweight =
4 M ×
N
h
, bias=
4
N
h
× 1
. Similar to the LSTM network structure used for estimating SoH, Fig. 12 illustrates the LSTM network structure used for estimating internal resistances.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the error of SoH and internal resistances estimation
ce:section.ce:para:
To evaluate the accuracy of SoH estimation and internal resistances, the paper utilizes the following evaluation criteria:
•
Mean Absolute Percentage Error -MAPE:
(9)
MAPE
%
=
100
N
∑
k = 1
K
x
k
−
x
ˆ
( k )
x ( k )
where
x
k
,
x
ˆ
( k )
respectively are real value and estimated value at test cycle k , N is total of test cycles.
•
Mean Absolute Error - MAE:
(10)
MAPE
%
=
1
N
∑
k = 1
K
x
k
−
x
ˆ
( k )
x ( k )
•
Root Mean Square Error - RMSE:
(11)
RMSE =
1
N
∑
k = 1
K
x
k
−
x
ˆ
k
2

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results of SoH and internal resistances estimation

ce:section.ce:para:
After numerous experiments, the LSTM network with a partition size of
M = 35
and 50 hidden nodes for the σ and tanh networks was selected. The Adam optimization algorithm was employed to solve the optimization problem of the LSTM network. One advantage of this algorithm is its fast optimization search speed and higher estimation accuracy compared to the Sgdm and rmsprop algorithms.

The data is divided into 03 sets, including the training set, the validation set, and the test set in the ratio of 60 %, 20 %, and 20 %, respectively. These are reasonable ratios taken from our many tests to prevent the LSTM network from overfitting, measure accurately the model’s performance on unseen data, and to build robust and reliable machine learning models that can be generalize well on new data when it applied to the practice.

The training result and the estimated SoH for the test cell with an initial capacity of 1863mAh are 87.92 %. The LSTM network learning process has a total of 2250 iterations, a learning rate of 0.001, 750 epochs, ReLU activation function, and learning time of 1 min 46 s (performed on a computer with 8 GB RAM, Core I5 processor speed). In general, a higher number of iterations can improve the performance of the estimation LSTM network model, but it can also lead to overfitting. Therefore, it’s essential to monitor the validation loss and stop the training when it starts to increase. The learning rate determine the step size taken during gradient descent to update the LSTM network model parameters. A high learning rate can cause the LSTM model to overshoot the optimal solution, while a low learning rate can lead to slow convergence. The optimal learning rate analytically for a specific LSTM model on a specific data set can not be predetermined, so in this study the learning rate is discovered through trial and error. We see that, the choice of the number of iterations, learning rate, and activation function can depend on the complexity of the cell SoH and internal resistances dynamic model, the size of input data and the LSTM model architecture.

The estimated SoH after network training is represented in Fig. 13, in which 95CI is 95 % confident interval. The error variation over the test cycle is shown in Fig. 14. The error in estimating SoH according to the criteria is: MAPE = 0.2585, MAE = 0.0041, RME = 0.0091. The Q-Q plot is shown in Fig. 15.

To demonstrate the advantages of LSTM networks in estimating State of Health (SoH), this paper compares the SoH estimation results of a cell using an FNN network with 50 hidden nodes, as shown in Fig. 16. Table 1 compares the error evaluation metrics of SoH estimation for cell 2 between the LSTM and FNN networks. The MAPE, MAE, and RMSE error metrics for SoH estimation using the LSTM network are smaller than those using the FNN network.

Specifically, for the MAPE metric, LSTM has a MAPE of 0.20812, while FNN has a MAPE of 0.57408. This means that the estimation error of FNN for SoH is 2.758 times larger than that of LSTM. For the MAE metric, LSTM has a MAE of 0.0034315, while FNN has a MAE of 0.0096357. This means that the MAE of FNN is 2.8 times larger than that of LSTM. For the RMSE metric, LSTM has a RMSE of 0.0082006, while FNN has a RMSE of 0.03264. This means that the RMSE of FNN is 3.98 times larger than that of LSTM. Additionally, the trend of poorer estimation accuracy of FNN is demonstrated when estimating the SoH for cells with longer usage time.

Fig. 17 shows the estimated value of
R
e
compared to the actual
R
e
of cell 2, and Table 2 shows the values of the error assessment indicators for the estimation of
R
e
. Similarly, Fig. 18 and Table 3 show the estimated values of
R
ct
and the error assessment indicators for the estimation of
R
ct
for cell 2.

Fig. 17 shows that the estimated resistance
R
e
is relatively accurate with MAPE = 0.39021, MAE = 0.00026402, and RMSE = 0.00047547. The estimated values by LSTM also closely match the sudden changes in the
R
e
values during the cycles, and the estimation errors remain stable without increasing over the test cycles. Similarly, the results of the estimated
R
ct
shown in Fig. 18 have MAPE = 0.35901, MAE = 0.00032711, and RMSE = 0.00062018. The error for the estimation of
R
ct
are larger than those for
R
e
, which may be due to the complex electrochemical phenomena affecting the electrical resistance, which are not fully reflected in the data characteristics.

The training results of the LSTM network and estimation results of SoH and resistance for the test cells using 03 performance metrics MAPE, MAE, and RMSE indicate that the LSTM network with 50 hidden nodes can estimate SoH with higher accuracy than the FNN network. For the estimation of resistance, the estimation errors are larger than for SoH due to the complex characteristics of the electrochemical process that are difficult to fully reflect through the data characteristics.

The relationship between State of Health (SoH) and the internal resistances within a cell is a non-linear relationship with complex characteristics. In reality, they are often estimated independently with various datasets, especially with data related to electrochemical impedance collected by specialized devices. The sequential estimation structure of SoH and then internal resistances proposed in this paper allows for the estimation of two parameters from a dataset comprising voltage, current, and temperature in charge/discharge scenarios. SoH estimation plays a supplementary input role with the initial input dataset used to estimate internal resistances, reducing the scale and characteristics of the data. The relationship between SoH and internal resistances is considered in the LSTM deep learning process, which enhances the accuracy of reflecting the relationship between SoH and internal resistances. Compared with the results estimated by FNN, this is a highly accurate method for estimating SoH [35,36], with higher accuracy shown in the three comparison indices of MAPE, MAE, and RMSE. The reason is that FNN and RNN models are prone to time-dependent bias due to the vanishing phenomenon, which reduces the accuracy of the SoH estimate, while the LSTM network with the learning mechanism in this study overcomes this phenomenon by adding additional interactions for cells to remember input information in the long term.

This paper applies LSTM and deep learning to estimate the SoH and internal resistances, specifically the SEI layer resistance and the transmission resistance of Lithium-ion battery cells. Using test data from charge/discharge scenarios including current, voltage, and temperature, the SoH of the battery cell is estimated by the first LSTM, and the internal resistances are estimated by the second LSTM along with the charge/discharge scenario data and the measured resistance. With this structure, the estimation of internal resistances becomes simpler and does not require capacitance and impedance spectroscopy measurement in practice. Comparison of the estimation errors using LSTM and FNN using three performance metrics MAPE, MPE, and RMSE shows that the estimation of SoH and internal resistances by LSTM is more accurate than the estimation by FNN.

Although the proposed model has advantages in terms of the accuracy of SoH estimation and the ability to sequentially estimate 02 SoH parameters and internal resistances, there are some limitations. Firstly, in this study, we only used 02 cells with the same type of battery, and the data for each cell was divided into three datasets: training, evaluation, and testing, with the same charge/discharge scenarios. Therefore, the sustainability of the model has not been thoroughly investigated. Furthermore, the accuracy of the SoH and resistance estimation can be further improved by adding a convolutional neural network mechanism to the LSTM network.

The estimation of SoC with the equivalent circuit model using the parameters of SoH and internal resistances will be the future works for us in the future. Using the estimated SoH and internal resistances as parameters of the updated equivalent circuit model in the long term, estimating other parameters in the BMS system will become easier and more accurate, one of which is the estimation of SoC. Estimating SoC as a short-term varying parameter by using the equivalent circuit model is also more physically meaningful as the SEI aging process is usually coupled with battery electrochemistry, which is reflected through the estimation of SoH and internal resistances.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported in part by the Thai Nguyen University of Technology, Viet nam.
